schedules.scheduleName: Schedule BP – Computation of Income from Business or Profession (Firm/LLP)
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Mandatory for all ITR-5 filers with business income.
schedules.description: Core schedule computing net business income of the firm. Starts with book profit, applies IT Act adjustments, and arrives at taxable income. Partner salary, interest, and remuneration allowable u/s 40(b) are deducted here from the firm's taxable income.
keyFields: Net profit as per P&L account
Add: Disallowances u/s 40A(3) – excess cash payments above ₹10,000/day
Add: Disallowances u/s 43B – taxes/PF/ESI not paid on time
Add: Disallowances u/s 43B(h) – MSME payments beyond 45 days outstanding
Less: IT Act depreciation u/s 32 (from Schedule DPM)
Less: Partner interest allowable u/s 40(b) (max 12% p.a. on capital)
Book Profit for Section 40(b) cap computation
Net business income of firm after all adjustments
commonMistakes: Not computing book profit separately for Section 40(b) cap verification
Claiming partner interest at more than 12% per annum
Deducting partner salary in excess of 40(b) limit
Forgetting that unregistered firms cannot deduct partner salary/interest
Not adding back partner salary before arriving at book profit (circular computation error)

schedules.scheduleName: Schedule 40(b) – Partner Remuneration and Interest Deduction Computation
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Required when the firm pays salary, remuneration, commission, or interest to partners and claims deduction u/s 40(b).
schedules.description: Detailed computation of partner remuneration allowable under Section 40(b). Book profit is net profit AFTER adding back the partner remuneration (circular computation avoided). The 40(b) cap: for book profit up to ₹6 lakh – lower of ₹3 lakh or 90% of book profit; for book profit exceeding ₹6 lakh – ₹3 lakh plus 60% of excess. For loss situations, minimum ₹1.5 lakh is deductible.
keyFields: Name of each partner and PAN
Whether working partner or sleeping partner
Remuneration paid as per partnership deed
Book profit computation (net profit + partner remuneration added back)
40(b) limit calculation: up to ₹6L book profit: ₹3L or 90% (higher); above ₹6L: 60%
Allowable remuneration (lower of actual or 40(b) limit)
Interest on capital paid to each partner (rate and amount)
Allowable interest (max 12% p.a. on opening + proportionate additions)
crossReferences: Partner's individual ITR-3 (salary from firm must match Schedule IF)
commonMistakes: Not having salary/interest authorized in the partnership deed (oral authorization insufficient)
Paying salary to non-working (sleeping) partners and claiming deduction
Calculating 40(b) limit on pre-salary net profit instead of book profit
Paying interest at rate exceeding 12% and claiming full deduction
Not deducting TDS u/s 194T on partner salary/interest (mandatory from 1 April 2024)

schedules.compulsoryCondition: Mandatory for all partnership firms, LLPs, AOPs, and BOIs.
schedules.description: Details of all partners/members including profit-sharing ratios, capital contributions, and income received from the entity. This data is cross-verified by the income tax system against partners' individual ITR-3 filings. For LLPs, designated partners and all other partners are disclosed.
keyFields: Whether working partner or sleeping partner
Share of profit (exempt u/s 10(2A) in partner's hands)
Changes in profit-sharing ratio (reconstitution events with dates)
Retirement or induction of partner during FY
commonMistakes: Not updating profit-sharing ratio when it changes mid-year (split income for each ratio period)
Incorrect closing capital not reconciling with opening + profit share – drawings
Omitting retiring or newly inducted partners
Not disclosing NRI partners' status (withholding tax implications)

schedules.scheduleName: Schedule P&L – Profit and Loss Account (Firm/LLP)
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Mandatory when firm maintains books of accounts (required for all registered firms).
schedules.description: Complete P&L account of the firm for FY 2025-26. Shows gross turnover, cost of goods sold, gross profit, all operating expenses including partner remuneration as booked, depreciation, interest expense, and net profit. Partner salaries appear as expense in P&L but are subjected to 40(b) cap in Schedule BP.
keyFields: Salaries and remuneration to partners (before 40(b) cap)
Interest paid to banks and others
commonMistakes: Mixing firm's income with partners' personal income
Not showing partner remuneration as expense in P&L
GST inclusive vs exclusive turnover confusion

schedules.compulsoryCondition: Mandatory when firm maintains books of accounts.
schedules.description: Complete Balance Sheet of the firm as at 31 March 2026. Partners' capital accounts on the liabilities side. Fixed assets, stocks, debtors, cash, bank on assets side. Each partner's capital account closing balance must reconcile with Schedule PI.
keyFields: Partners' capital accounts (partner-wise with opening and closing balances)
Secured loans (bank OD, CC, term loans)
commonMistakes: Not segregating MSME payables (required for 43B(h) compliance)
Partners' closing capital not matching Schedule PI
Including personal assets of partners in firm Balance Sheet

schedules.scheduleName: Schedule DPM – Depreciation on Plant and Machinery (Firm)
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Required when firm owns any depreciable assets.
schedules.description: Block-wise depreciation computation as per Income Tax Act for all firm assets. Opening WDV, additions, disposals, and closing WDV for each asset block. IT Act rates apply (WDV method).
keyFields: Block description and IT Act depreciation rate
Additions during year (full or half-year as applicable)
Normal depreciation and additional depreciation u/s 32(1)(iia)
commonMistakes: Using SLM instead of WDV method
Full year depreciation on assets added after 3 October 2025
Not accounting for terminal depreciation when asset block becomes zero

schedules.scheduleName: Schedule CG – Capital Gains (Firm's Capital Assets)
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Required when the firm sells any capital asset during FY 2025-26.
schedules.description: Capital gains of the firm from sale of investments, immovable property, goodwill, or other capital assets. Firm is taxed at the same capital gains rates as individuals: STCG 111A at 20%, LTCG 112A at 12.5% above ₹1.25 lakh, LTCG on property at 20% with indexation.
keyFields: Asset description, date of acquisition, date of sale
Indexed cost of acquisition (for LTCG assets)
Exemptions u/s 54EC (₹50 lakh cap in NHAI/REC bonds)
crossReferences: Schedule BP (gains on sale of depreciable assets – treated as STCG u/s 50)
commonMistakes: Treating sale of depreciable assets as LTCG (always STCG u/s 50 regardless of holding period)
Missing 54EC exemption on LTCG from property sale

schedules.scheduleName: Schedule AMT – Alternate Minimum Tax u/s 115JC
schedules.compulsoryCondition: Required when firm/LLP claims deductions u/s 10AA or 80H to 80RRB, and adjusted total income exceeds ₹20 lakh.
schedules.description: AMT ensures firms claiming profit-linked deductions pay at least 18.5% of adjusted total income. AMT credit can be carried forward for 15 years to offset normal tax in future years.
keyFields: Adjusted total income (total income + deductions added back)
Surcharge on AMT (12% if adjusted income > ₹1 crore)
AMT credit brought forward from earlier years
Net tax payable after AMT credit
commonMistakes: Not computing AMT when SEZ deduction u/s 10AA is claimed
Forgetting AMT credit from prior years (can significantly reduce current year tax)

schedules.compulsoryCondition: Applicable only to cooperative societies filing ITR-5.
schedules.description: Section 80P provides deductions for cooperative societies on various types of income. Primary agricultural credit societies, credit cooperatives, and other specified cooperative societies get different deduction percentages. The entire income from banking activities, cottage industries, and certain marketing activities can be deducted.
keyFields: Income from banking/credit activities to members
Income from supply of agricultural implements
Whether opting for 115BAD (22% rate) instead of 80P
crossReferences: Part B-TTI (Tax computation at special rate or normal rate)
commonMistakes: Multi-state cooperative societies claiming 80P (restricted after SC ruling in Citizen Co-op Society)
Claiming 80P while also opting for 115BAD (mutually exclusive)
Not segregating income from members vs non-members
